sentences.base_language: Lucas had been planning the move for months and the day had finally arrived.
sentences.target_language: Lucas planifiait le déménagement depuis des mois et le jour était enfin arrivé.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Planifiait depuis** — **depuis** + imparfait expresses an action that had been ongoing up to a past reference point: **planifiait depuis des mois** (had been planning for months). In English this is a past perfect continuous; French uses the imparfait + **depuis** instead. Compare: **depuis** + present tense for an action still ongoing at the moment of speaking: **il planifie depuis des mois** (he has been planning for months — and still is). The imparfait + **depuis** shifts the same logic into the past.
**Était enfin arrivé** — plus-que-parfait of **arriver** (with **être**): **était arrivé** (had finally arrived). Here the plus-que-parfait expresses a completed arrival whose result — the day being here — is what matters at this narrative moment. Past participle **arrivé** agrees with the subject **le jour** (masculine singular — no added ending). The adverb **enfin** (finally, at last) is placed between auxiliary and past participle: **était enfin arrivé**.

sentences.base_language: His friends arrived early in the morning to help carry boxes and furniture down to the moving truck.
sentences.target_language: Ses amis sont arrivés tôt le matin pour aider à porter les cartons et les meubles jusqu'au camion de déménagement.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Sont arrivés** — **arriver** takes **être** as auxiliary in the passé composé; past participle agrees with the subject: **sont arrivés** (they arrived — masculine plural **-s**). The core group of verbs taking **être** (**arriver, partir, entrer, sortir, monter, descendre, naître, mourir**, and all pronominal verbs) require agreement of the past participle with the grammatical subject: **elles sont arrivées** (f. pl.), **il est arrivé** (m. sg.).
**Pour aider à + infinitif** = in order to help (to do): **pour aider à porter** (to help carry). **Aider à** + infinitive = to help do something: **aider à** porter (help carry), **aider à** déménager (help move). Compare: **aider quelqu'un à** + infinitive (with a person as object): **aider Lucas à** porter les cartons (help Lucas carry the boxes). **Jusqu'à** = as far as, all the way to, down to: **jusqu'au** camion (down to the truck) — **jusqu'à** + definite article contracts: **jusqu'au** (= **jusqu'à** + **le**).

sentences.base_language: After several trips up and down the stairs, everyone's arms and legs were aching.
sentences.target_language: Après plusieurs allers-retours dans les escaliers, tout le monde avait les bras et les jambes qui faisaient mal.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avait les bras… qui faisaient mal** — the construction **avoir** + body part + **qui** + verb describes a physical sensation attached to part of the body: **avait les bras qui faisaient mal** (had arms that were aching). This is more idiomatic than **ses bras lui faisaient mal**, though both are correct. **Faire mal** = to ache, to hurt: **les jambes qui font mal** (legs that ache). The imparfait **faisaient** presents the aching as an ongoing background state.
**Tout le monde** + singular verb — **tout le monde** (everyone) is grammatically singular and always takes a third-person singular verb: **tout le monde avait** (everyone had), **tout le monde est parti** (everyone left). This is a common source of error for English speakers who think of 'everyone' as plural. **Plusieurs** = several — an invariable adjective always used with a plural noun: **plusieurs allers-retours** (several round trips). **Aller-retour** (m.) = round trip, back-and-forth trip; plural: **allers-retours**.

sentences.base_language: The old apartment looked strangely empty once everything had been removed.
sentences.target_language: Le vieil appartement paraissait étrangement vide une fois que tout avait été enlevé.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Une fois que + plus-que-parfait** = once (something had happened): **une fois que tout avait été enlevé** (once everything had been removed). The plus-que-parfait in the subordinate clause marks an action completed before the main past action (the apartment looking empty). This temporal subordinator + plus-que-parfait combination is the standard way to express 'once X had happened, Y was the case' in French narrative.
**Avait été enlevé** — passive voice in the plus-que-parfait: **avait été** + past participle **enlevé** (had been removed). The passive is formed with **être** (conjugated) + past participle, which agrees with the subject: **tout** is masculine singular, so **enlevé** has no added ending. Compare with the active plus-que-parfait: **on avait tout enlevé** (one/they had removed everything) — French often prefers **on** + active voice over the passive. **Paraître** + adjective = to look, to seem: **paraissait vide** (looked empty) — more literary than **sembler** or **avoir l'air**.

sentences.base_language: Lucas took one last look around and felt a wave of nostalgia mixed with excitement about what lay ahead.
sentences.target_language: Lucas a jeté un dernier regard autour de lui et a ressenti une vague de nostalgie mêlée d'excitation à l'idée de ce qui l'attendait.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Jeter un regard** = to take a look, to cast a glance: **a jeté un dernier regard** (took one last look). **Jeter un regard** is more expressive than the simple **regarder**; it conveys a deliberate, momentary glance. Related expressions: **jeter un coup d'œil** (to take a quick look — very common and informal), **jeter un regard en arrière** (to look back — literal or figurative).
**À l'idée de** + infinitive or noun = at the thought of, about: **à l'idée de ce qui l'attendait** (at the thought of what lay ahead). **À l'idée de** is a fixed prepositional phrase expressing the mental trigger for an emotion: **à l'idée de** partir (at the thought of leaving), **à l'idée de** l'inconnu (at the thought of the unknown). **Ce qui l'attendait** = what awaited him — relative pronoun **ce qui** (what, that which) functions as the subject of the subordinate clause: **ce qui** l'attendait (what lay ahead / what awaited him). **Mêlé/e de** = mixed with: **nostalgie mêlée d'**excitation (nostalgia mixed with excitement).

sentences.base_language: The new apartment was on the third floor of a quiet building in a lively neighborhood close to the city center.
sentences.target_language: Le nouvel appartement se trouvait au troisième étage d'un immeuble calme dans un quartier animé, à deux pas du centre-ville.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se trouver** = to be located, to be situated: **se trouvait au troisième étage** (was on the third floor). **Se trouver** (pronominal) is the standard verb for location of places: **l'appartement se trouve** au troisième étage (the apartment is on the third floor). Compare: **être** (to be — also possible but more neutral), **se situer** (to be situated — slightly more formal). For floor numbers, French counts from the ground floor (**rez-de-chaussée**), so **troisième étage** = what English calls the 4th floor.
**À deux pas de** = very close to, just around the corner from: **à deux pas du** centre-ville (a stone's throw from the city center / close to the city center). **À deux pas de** is an idiomatic expression meaning nearby — literally 'two steps from'. Compare: **à proximité de** (close to — neutral), **près de** (near — neutral), **à deux pas de** (very close — idiomatic and vivid). **Nouvel** — the masculine singular adjective **nouveau** changes to **nouvel** before a vowel or mute h: **nouvel** appartement (before vowel). Compare: **un nouveau** quartier (before consonant) vs **un nouvel** ami (before vowel).

sentences.base_language: When they arrived, a neighbor from down the hall came out to introduce herself and welcome Lucas warmly.
sentences.target_language: À leur arrivée, une voisine du couloir est sortie pour se présenter et accueillir Lucas chaleureusement.
sentences.grammarNote:
**À leur arrivée** = upon their arrival, when they arrived: **à leur arrivée** (at their arrival). The prepositional phrase **à** + possessive + verbal noun is a compact, elegant alternative to a temporal clause: **à leur arrivée** (when they arrived), **à sa sortie** (when he left / upon leaving). This construction is very common in formal and written French.
**Est sortie** — **sortir** takes **être** in the passé composé; past participle agrees with subject: **est sortie** (came out — feminine **-e** agreeing with **une voisine**). **Sortir** with **être** = to come out, to go out (intransitive). When **sortir** is transitive (with a direct object), it takes **avoir**: elle **a sorti** ses clés (she took out her keys). **Se présenter** = to introduce oneself (pronominal): **se présenter** (to introduce oneself) vs **présenter quelqu'un** (to introduce someone else). **Chaleureusement** (warmly) is the adverb of **chaleureux/chaleureuse** (warm, warm-hearted) — formed by adding **-ment** to the feminine adjective: **chaleureuse** → **chaleureusement**.

sentences.base_language: She told him there was an excellent bakery and a small fresh market just around the corner.
sentences.target_language: Elle lui a dit qu'il y avait une excellente boulangerie et un petit marché frais juste au coin de la rue.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a dit** — indirect object pronoun **lui** (to him/her) placed before the conjugated verb: **elle lui a dit** (she told him). When the verb **dire** has both a direct object (the content of what is said, introduced by **que**) and an indirect object (the person told), the indirect object is expressed by the pronoun **lui** (singular) or **leur** (plural): **il lui a dit** (he told him/her), **elle leur a dit** (she told them). The pronoun precedes the auxiliary in the passé composé.
**Qu'il y avait** — reported speech: **a dit que** + imparfait. The original statement was **il y a** (there is — present), but in reported speech after a past main verb, the present becomes the imparfait: **il y a** → **il y avait** (concordance des temps). **Il y avait** = there was/were (imparfait of **il y a**): **il y avait une boulangerie** (there was a bakery). **Juste au coin de la rue** = just around the corner: **au coin de la rue** (at/on the corner of the street) — a very common expression for indicating proximity.

sentences.base_language: Unloading the truck and carrying everything up the stairs took the rest of the afternoon.
sentences.target_language: Décharger le camion et monter tout par les escaliers a pris le reste de l'après-midi.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Infinitif sujet** — two infinitives used as the subject of the sentence: **décharger… et monter…** a pris (unloading… and carrying… took). In French, the infinitive can serve as the subject of a verb, often where English would use a gerund (-ing form): **partir tôt** est conseillé (leaving early is advised). When two infinitives are coordinated as subject, the verb is typically singular: **décharger et monter** **a** pris (took — singular agreement).
**A pris** — **prendre** in the passé composé: **a pris** (took). **Prendre** is used in the sense of 'to take (time or effort)': **ça a pris** deux heures (it took two hours). **Monter par les escaliers** = to carry up the stairs, to go up the stairs: **monter par les escaliers** (go/carry up via the stairs) — **par** here indicates the route or means. Compare: **monter dans** l'ascenseur (go up in the elevator). **Le reste de** = the rest of: **le reste de l'**après-midi (the rest of the afternoon).

sentences.base_language: By the time the heavy furniture was in place, everyone was exhausted and soaked in sweat.
sentences.target_language: Lorsque les meubles lourds ont été installés, tout le monde était épuisé et trempé de sueur.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lorsque + passé composé / imparfait** — **lorsque** (when) + passé composé marks the completed foreground event, while the imparfait in the main clause describes the resulting state: **lorsque… ont été installés** (when… had been put in place) / **était épuisé** (was exhausted — state). This tense pairing — subordinate passé composé + main clause imparfait — is a standard narrative structure expressing 'when X was done, Y was the condition'.
**Ont été installés** — passive voice in the passé composé: **ont été** + past participle **installés** (were put in place). The past participle agrees with the subject **les meubles** (masculine plural): **installés** (with **-s**). The passive is formed with **être** + past participle; in compound tenses, **être** is conjugated in the target tense and the past participle still agrees with the subject. **Trempé de sueur** = soaked in sweat: **trempé de** (soaked in/with) + noun. Compare: **trempé jusqu'aux os** (soaked to the bone — idiomatic), **en sueur** (in a sweat — also common).

sentences.base_language: Lucas ordered several large pizzas to thank his friends for all their help.
sentences.target_language: Lucas a commandé plusieurs grandes pizzas pour remercier ses amis de tout leur aide.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pour + infinitif** expressing purpose = in order to, to: **pour remercier** ses amis (to thank his friends). **Pour** + infinitive expresses the purpose or aim of the main action: **il est sorti pour acheter** du pain (he went out to buy bread). The subject of the infinitive must be the same as that of the main verb. When the subjects differ, **pour que** + subjunctive is used instead: **il a commandé des pizzas pour que ses amis soient contents** (he ordered pizzas so that his friends would be happy).
**Remercier quelqu'un de quelque chose** = to thank someone for something: **remercier ses amis de** tout leur aide (thank his friends for all their help). **Remercier** takes **de** before a noun or infinitive: **remercier de** son aide (thank for the help), **remercier d'**être venu (thank for coming). Compare with **remercier pour** (also used colloquially, especially before a noun): **merci pour** ton aide (thanks for your help — informal). In formal written French, **de** is preferred. Note: **aide** (f.) = help; **de tout leur aide** = for all their help.

sentences.base_language: They sat on the floor surrounded by cardboard boxes and ate ravenously, too tired to search for the plates.
sentences.target_language: Ils se sont assis par terre, entourés de cartons, et ont mangé avec voracité, trop fatigués pour chercher les assiettes.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se sont assis** — pronominal verb **s'asseoir** (to sit down) with **être** in the passé composé: **se sont assis** (they sat down — masculine plural, no added ending on **assis** as it already ends in **-s**). **S'asseoir** is an irregular pronominal verb: present **je m'assieds / je m'assois**, passé composé **je me suis assis/e**. Compare: **être assis** (to be seated — state, using **être** + adjective) vs **s'asseoir** (to sit down — action). **Par terre** = on the floor, on the ground: **s'asseoir par terre** (sit on the floor) — **par terre** is used for a flat surface underfoot; **sur le sol** is more neutral/formal.
**Trop + adjectif + pour + infinitif** = too (adjective) to (do): **trop fatigués pour chercher** (too tired to search). This construction expresses a reason for not doing something: **trop occupé pour répondre** (too busy to reply), **trop loin pour marcher** (too far to walk). **Entourés de** = surrounded by — past participle used as adjective, agreeing with **ils** (masculine plural): **entourés de** cartons. Note: **entouré de** (surrounded by) takes **de** not **par**, unlike passive voice constructions.

sentences.base_language: After his friends left, Lucas stood alone in the middle of the new space for the first time.
sentences.target_language: Après que ses amis furent partis, Lucas se retrouva seul au milieu du nouvel espace pour la première fois.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Après que + plus-que-parfait (or subjonctif passé)** — after a completed action: **après que ses amis furent partis** uses the passé antérieur (a literary tense equivalent to plus-que-parfait in subordinate clauses after **après que**, **dès que**, **quand** in literary narrative). In everyday French, **après que** + plus-que-parfait is also used: **après que ses amis étaient partis**. Grammarians insist **après que** takes the indicative (not subjunctive), unlike **avant que** which takes the subjunctive.
**Se retrouver** = to find oneself, to end up (in a state or place): **se retrouva seul** (found himself alone). **Se retrouver** (pronominal) is frequently used to describe ending up in a situation: **se retrouver seul** (find oneself alone), **se retrouver sans argent** (find oneself without money). It implies an arrival at a state, often somewhat unexpected. **Au milieu de** = in the middle of: **au milieu du** nouvel espace (in the middle of the new space). **Pour la première fois** = for the first time — a fixed adverbial phrase placed at the end of the clause.

sentences.base_language: The rooms felt unfamiliar and full of echoes but somehow also full of possibility.
sentences.target_language: Les pièces semblaient étrangères et pleines d'échos, mais aussi, d'une certaine façon, pleines de possibilités.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Sembler + adjectif** = to feel, to seem: **semblaient étrangères** (felt unfamiliar — lit. seemed foreign). **Sembler** + adjective is the standard construction for expressing how something appears or feels: **la situation semble difficile** (the situation seems difficult). The imparfait **semblaient** is used here for an ongoing state or impression, contrasting with the passé composé which would imply a punctual event. The adjective **étrangère** (foreign, unfamiliar, strange — feminine plural **étrangères**) agrees with **les pièces** (f. pl.).
**D'une certaine façon** = somehow, in a way: **d'une certaine façon** (somehow — lit. in a certain way). This adverbial phrase softens a statement, implying an impression that is hard to define precisely: **d'une certaine façon**, tout s'est bien passé (somehow, everything went well). Compare: **d'une façon ou d'une autre** (one way or another), **en quelque sorte** (in a way, so to speak — slightly more formal). **Plein/e de** + noun = full of: **pleines de** possibilités (full of possibilities) — **plein** agrees with the noun it modifies: **pleines** (f. pl.) with **les pièces**.

sentences.base_language: He decided to start by unpacking the kitchen — that always made a new place feel more like home.
sentences.target_language: Il a décidé de commencer par déballer la cuisine — ça rendait toujours un nouvel endroit plus proche de chez soi.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Décider de + infinitif** = to decide to do: **a décidé de commencer** (decided to start). **Décider de** + infinitive is the standard construction: **décider de** partir (decide to leave), **décider de** rester (decide to stay). Compare: **décider que** + indicative (to decide that — introducing a fact): **il a décidé qu'il partirait** (he decided that he would leave). **Commencer par** + infinitive = to start by doing, to begin with: **commencer par** déballer la cuisine (start by unpacking the kitchen). **Commencer par** indicates the first step in a sequence: **commençons par** le commencement (let's start from the beginning).
**Ça rendait… plus proche de chez soi** — **rendre** + object + adjective = to make (something) + adjective: **ça rendait** un endroit **plus proche de chez soi** (it made a place feel more like home). **Rendre** is used to express transformation: **rendre heureux** (make happy), **rendre difficile** (make difficult). The imparfait **rendait** expresses a habitual truth: it always had that effect. **Chez soi** = at home, one's own place — **chez soi** is the reflexive form of **chez** + disjunctive pronoun used for general or impersonal reference: **se sentir chez soi** (to feel at home).

sentences.base_language: Finding the box labeled 'bedding' turned into a twenty-minute search and very nearly caused a moment of panic.
sentences.target_language: Trouver le carton étiqueté « literie » s'est transformé en une recherche de vingt minutes et a bien failli provoquer un moment de panique.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'est transformé en** = turned into, became: **s'est transformé en une recherche** (turned into a search). **Se transformer en** (pronominal) = to turn into, to change into: **la chenille se transforme en** papillon (the caterpillar turns into a butterfly), **la situation s'est transformée en** chaos (the situation turned into chaos). Past participle **transformé** agrees with the subject **trouver le carton** — the infinitive phrase as subject is treated as masculine singular, so no added ending.
**Avoir failli + infinitif** = to very nearly do, to almost do (but ultimately not): **a bien failli provoquer** (very nearly caused). **Faillir** + infinitive expresses a near-miss: **j'ai failli tomber** (I nearly fell), **elle a failli rater son train** (she almost missed her train). The adverb **bien** intensifies **failli**: **a bien failli** (very nearly, came very close to). **Faillir** is irregular and used almost exclusively in compound tenses. **Étiqueté** = labeled — past participle of **étiqueter** used as adjective: **le carton étiqueté** « literie » (the box labeled 'bedding'). **Literie** (f.) = bedding, bed linen.

sentences.base_language: By midnight, the bed was assembled and the essentials were in place.
sentences.target_language: À minuit, le lit était monté et l'essentiel était en place.
sentences.grammarNote:
**À minuit** = at midnight — **à** + time expression: **à minuit** (at midnight), **à midi** (at noon), **à huit heures** (at eight o'clock). For 'by midnight' (indicating a deadline or limit), French uses **à** or **avant minuit** depending on nuance: **à minuit** (at midnight — at that moment), **avant minuit** (before midnight — strictly before). In narrative context, **à minuit** here functions as 'by midnight' — the state achieved at that point.
**Était monté** — imparfait of the passive **être monté** or, more naturally, **être** + past participle used as a state (statal passive): **le lit était monté** (the bed was assembled — describing the resulting state). **Monter** un lit = to assemble/put up a bed. In French, the distinction between the dynamic passive (**le lit a été monté** — the bed was assembled [at a point in time]) and the statal passive / resultant state (**le lit était monté** — the bed was [in the assembled state]) is expressed through tense choice: passé composé for the action, imparfait for the resulting state. **L'essentiel** (m.) = the essentials, the essential things — a nominalized adjective: **l'essentiel** est en place (the essentials are in place).

sentences.base_language: Lucas lay down exhausted, listening to the unfamiliar sounds of the building and the street outside.
sentences.target_language: Lucas s'est allongé épuisé, en écoutant les bruits inconnus de l'immeuble et de la rue dehors.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En écoutant** — gérondif expressing a simultaneous action: **en écoutant les bruits** (while listening to the sounds). The gérondif (**en** + present participle) indicates that Lucas listened while lying down — two actions happening at the same time by the same subject. Formation: take the **nous**-form of the present tense, drop **-ons**, add **-ant**: **écout-ons** → **écoutant** → **en écoutant**. The three irregular gérondifs to remember: **en étant** (être), **en ayant** (avoir), **en sachant** (savoir).
**S'est allongé** — pronominal verb **s'allonger** (to lie down, to stretch out) with **être** in the passé composé: **s'est allongé** (lay down — masculine, no added ending). **S'allonger** = to lie down, to stretch out: **s'allonger sur le canapé** (to lie down on the sofa). Compare: **être allongé** (to be lying down — state): **il était allongé** (he was lying down). **Épuisé** = exhausted — past participle of **épuiser** used as adjective: **épuisé** (masc. sg.), **épuisée** (fem.), **épuisés** (m. pl.). Here it is an appositive adjective agreeing with **Lucas** (masculine singular).

sentences.base_language: A distant clock struck midnight and Lucas finally felt something settle inside him — this was home now.
sentences.target_language: Une horloge lointaine a sonné minuit et Lucas a enfin senti quelque chose se poser en lui — c'était chez lui, maintenant.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A senti quelque chose se poser** — perception verb **sentir** + infinitive: **a senti quelque chose se poser** (felt something settle). Verbs of perception (**voir, entendre, sentir, regarder, écouter, percevoir**) followed by a noun/pronoun + infinitive describe experiencing an action take place: **il a senti la fatigue le quitter** (he felt the tiredness leave him), **elle a entendu la porte claquer** (she heard the door slam). The noun (**quelque chose**) is the logical subject of the infinitive (**se poser**).
**C'était chez lui** = this was home, this was his place: **c'était chez lui** (it was home for him — lit. it was at his place). **Chez lui/elle** = at his/her place, his/her home: **rentrer chez lui** (go home), **être chez soi** (be at home). Used with **c'est/c'était**, the expression conveys belonging: **c'est chez moi** (this is my home). **Se poser** (pronominal) = to settle, to land, to come to rest: **quelque chose s'est posé en lui** (something settled inside him) — a figurative use of **se poser** (literally: to land, as a bird). **Sonner** + time = to strike (of a clock): **sonner minuit** (to strike midnight).

sentences.base_language: As he drifted off to sleep, he felt grateful for his good friends and genuinely excited about everything the new neighborhood had in store.
sentences.target_language: En s'endormant, il se sentait reconnaissant envers ses bons amis et sincèrement enthousiaste à l'idée de tout ce que le nouveau quartier lui réservait.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En s'endormant** — gérondif of the pronominal verb **s'endormir**: **en s'endormant** (as he drifted off to sleep / while falling asleep). For pronominal verbs, the reflexive pronoun agrees with the implied subject in the gérondif: **en me réveillant** (upon waking up), **en nous levant** (as we got up), **en s'endormant** (as he/she drifted off). The gérondif here expresses simultaneity — the feeling of gratitude occurred as he was falling asleep.
**Ce que le quartier lui réservait** = what the neighborhood had in store for him: **ce que… lui réservait** (what it was reserving/holding in store for him). **Réserver** in this figurative sense = to have in store: **l'avenir lui réserve des surprises** (the future has surprises in store for him). **Lui** is the indirect object pronoun (for him/her). **Ce que** = what, that which — **ce que** functions as the object of the verb: **ce que** l'avenir réserve (what the future holds). Compare: **ce qui** (what — as subject), **ce que** (what — as object). **Envers** = toward, with regard to (for feelings or attitudes): **reconnaissant envers** ses amis (grateful to/toward his friends) — **envers** is used with attitudes and feelings directed at people.
